description: This figure comprises four sub-panels presenting the microstructural characteristics and mechanical properties of multiphase low-density steels (Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si) subjected to intercritical annealing at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C followed by low-temperature bainitic aging at 400°C (designated AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T). Panel (a) displays bar charts of volume fractions for four distinct phases: ferrite (F), bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM), fresh martensite (FM), and retained austenite (RA). As annealing temperature increases from 750°C to 850°C, RA content drastically decreases from 7.4% to 0.6%, FM reduces from 8.3% to 3.3%, while F fraction initially decreases then increases (49.3% → 46.8% → 54.7%). Panel (b) shows the corresponding average grain/phase diameters: F coarsens from 4.53 μm to 5.35 μm, whereas B/TM refines significantly to 0.86 μm at 850°C. Panel (c) presents engineering stress-strain curves, revealing that AN800-T achieves the highest ultimate tensile strength (1295 MPa) with 18.7% elongation, while AN750-T exhibits superior ductility (34.0% elongation) with tensile strength of 1068 MPa; AN850-T shows reduced elongation (13.9%) despite high strength (1269 MPa). Panel (d) compares the strength-ductility balance against conventional advanced high-strength steels (QP, DP, TRIP, TBF), demonstrating that the present steel achieves an outstanding product of strength and elongation (PSE) exceeding 30 GPa·%, positioning it above typical performance envelopes. The figure collectively establishes that the heterogeneous multiphase microstructure, particularly the optimized retention of RA (7.4%) and refined B/TM domains in AN750-T, enables effective strain partitioning and back-stress strengthening, delivering exceptional mechanical properties through the TRIP effect and geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation at soft-hard phase boundaries.
